input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
**Physical Twin System (Incubator CPS):**
  - **Type of System:** Cyber-Physical System (CPS) focused on temperature regulation.
  - **Main Components:**
    - Insulated styrofoam container (for thermal insulation).
    - Heatbed for internal heating.
    - Fan for heat distribution within the box.
    - Temperature sensors (2 internal + 1 external) for monitoring.
    - Raspberry Pi as the central controller for real-time regulation.
  - **Operational Domain:** Controlled environment for maintaining a stable internal temperature regardless of external conditions or internal content variations.
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning:**
    - Dynamic thermal behavior influenced by environmental and internal factors.
    - Real-time sensor data availability for monitoring and feedback.
    - Need for predictive control to achieve precise temperature regulation.
    - Reconfigurability and robustness requirements due to variable internal content.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
**Digital Twin Services:**
  - **Monitoring:**
    - Real-time data collection from physical sensors (internal/external temperatures).
    - Continuous validation of system performance against target temperature thresholds.
  - **Optimization:**
    - Calibration algorithms updating models of the CPS and environment with parameter estimates.
    - Adaptive control strategies to optimize energy efficiency and temperature stability.
  - **Prediction:**
    - Simulation-based forecasting of system behavior under current operating conditions.
    - Anticipation of temperature deviations due to environmental changes or sensor noise.
  - **Control:**
    - Supervisory control via algorithms (e.g., state estimators, fault detectors) to ensure safe and optimal operation.
    - Human/algorithm-driven interventions for adjustments (e.g., adjusting heatbed/fan settings).
  - **Visualization:**
    - Advanced visualizations of system state (e.g., temperature maps, heat distribution dynamics).
    - Comparative analysis between physical and digital twin states for validation.
  - **Target Users:**
    - Engineers, operators, and researchers analyzing system performance, debugging, or optimizing temperature control.
    - Maintenance teams for predictive upkeep and fault detection.
  - **Service Capabilities:**
    - Robustness against environmental changes (e.g., external temperature fluctuations).
    - Reconfigurability for different internal content types.
    - Safety compliance monitoring via real-time anomaly detection.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
**Tooling and Enablers for Digital Twin Implementation:**
  - **Software Platforms:**
    - Raspberry Pi as the core cyber component for real-time control and data processing.
    - Custom calibration algorithms (implemented in the Incubator repository) to update models dynamically.
  - **Development Tools:**
    - Hardware-specific tools for sensor integration (e.g., temperature sensor drivers).
    - Simulation engines (e.g., differential equation models for dynamic behavior analysis).
  - **Simulation Engines:**
    - Mathematical models (e.g., differential equations) representing thermal dynamics, heat distribution, and sensor interactions.
    - Calibration methods (e.g., parameter estimation) to align digital models with physical behavior.
  - **Databases:**
    - Real-time data storage for sensor readings (internal/external temperatures).
    - Historical data repositories for trend analysis and predictive modeling.
  - **Specialized Technologies:**
    - **CPS Framework:** Integration of cyber (Raspberry Pi) and physical components (incubator hardware).
    - **Algorithmic Enablers:** Fault detection, state estimation, and supervisory control algorithms.
    - **Industry-Specific Tools:** Referenced repository ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) for hardware/software specifications and implementation details.
    - **Visualization Tools:** Likely custom or third-party tools for real-time dashboards (not explicitly named in documents).
  - **Frameworks/Standards:**
    - General Digital Twin frameworks (e.g., those integrating modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization).
    - Industry 4.0 principles for interoperability and automation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system operates with **real-time synchronization** between the physical and digital twins, leveraging the **Raspberry Pi** as the central controller. The temporal granularity is dictated by the **sampling rate of the temperature sensors** (two inside and one outside the insulated box), which are likely triggered at **low-to-moderate frequencies** (e.g., every few seconds to minutes) to monitor thermal dynamics. The **latency requirements** are implicit but tied to the **calibration and model updates**—the DT employs **parameterized thermal models** (e.g., differential equations with 2–4 free parameters) to predict temperature behavior, necessitating **real-time or near-real-time feedback loops** for accurate state estimation. The **time scales** for services include:
  - **Model simulation**: High-fidelity simulations (e.g., differential equation-based) may run at **higher abstraction levels** (e.g., seconds to minutes) for long-term predictions, while **low-fidelity calibration models** could operate at finer granularities (e.g., seconds).
  - **Monitoring/control**: The **Raspberry Pi controller** processes sensor data and triggers adjustments (e.g., heatbed/fan activation) within **millisecond-to-second latency** to maintain target temperature.
  The **twinning time scale** is thus a **hybrid of real-time (control loop) and near-real-time (model calibration)** operations, with sensor sampling and model updates as critical synchronization points.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The incubator Digital Twin system exhibits **single-instance architecture** for the core DT, where:
  - **One centralized DT instance** integrates the **physical twin (PT)**—the insulated incubator with sensors and actuators—and its **cyber-physical environment** (e.g., heatbed, fan, styrofoam insulation).
  - **No decentralized twins** are mentioned; the DT is a **unified representation** of the entire system, with **responsibilities centralized** around the Raspberry Pi controller and the DT’s calibration/optimization algorithms.
  - **Coordination mechanisms** are implicit but rely on:
    - **Temporal synchronization** (as described in `twinning_time_scale`).
    - **Model-data alignment** via **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation for thermal models) to ensure consistency between the PT and its digital counterpart.
  The **scope of the DT** is **end-to-end**: it encompasses the **physical plant** (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors) and its **environmental interactions** (e.g., thermal inertia of the heatbed, external temperature). Multiplicity is thus **one-to-one** between the PT and its DT, with no hierarchical or federated twins depicted.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **monolithic architecture pattern**, characterized by:
  - **Core components**:
    1. **Physical Twin (PT)**: The insulated incubator with hardware (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors) and the Raspberry Pi controller.
    2. **Digital Twin (DT)**: A **single, integrated model** of the PT, comprising:
       - **Two thermal models** (with 2 and 4 free parameters, respectively) for predicting temperature dynamics.
       - **Calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation) to update model parameters in real-time using sensor data.
       - **Monitoring modules** (e.g., fault detection, state estimation) that validate PT performance against DT predictions.
       - **Optimization algorithms** (implicitly for control decisions, e.g., adjusting heatbed/fan output).
    3. **Environmental models**: The DT includes a representation of the **external environment** (e.g., ambient temperature sensor) to account for thermal coupling.
  - **Integration patterns**:
    - **Direct coupling** between the PT’s sensors (temperature readings) and the DT’s models via **real-time data streams**.
    - **Feedback loops**: The DT’s predictions inform the Raspberry Pi’s control actions (e.g., adjusting heatbed/fan) to maintain target temperature.
    - **No explicit orchestration layer** is described beyond the Raspberry Pi’s role as the **central coordinator** between the PT and DT.
  - **Resource management**: The DT’s computational resources are **embedded within the Raspberry Pi’s cyber infrastructure**, with no mention of distributed or cloud-based orchestration. The **latency-sensitive operations** (e.g., control decisions) are prioritized over high-fidelity simulations.
  The constellation is **closed-loop**, with the DT acting as a **real-time predictive controller** for the PT’s thermal management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the thermal incubator system integrates **two simulation models** for temperature prediction:
1. A **basic model** with **two free parameters** (simpler, less accurate for thermal inertia).
2. A **comprehensive model** with **four free parameters**, explicitly designed to better capture the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**—making it the preferred choice for further DT development.

**Data Components**:
- **Data sources** are derived from **sensors** embedded in the physical incubator, collecting real-time temperature measurements.
- **Data management** leverages open-source hardware/software, ensuring accessibility for validation and verification of DT techniques.

**Model Relationships**:
- The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update **cyber-physical system (CPS) models** and environmental parameters using real-time sensor data, ensuring alignment between digital and physical twins.
- Models are **integrated hierarchically**: the four-parameter model is used for accurate thermal behavior prediction, while the two-parameter model serves as a baseline for comparative analysis.
- **Model fidelity** is validated through empirical experiments demonstrating superior predictive accuracy of the four-parameter model over the two-parameter one.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The incubator’s **sensing infrastructure** includes:
- **Temperature sensors** embedded within the heatbed and incubator environment, measuring spatial temperature distributions.
- **Open-source hardware** (not specified beyond availability) enables real-time data collection with **no explicit sampling frequency or transmission rate mentioned**, but the system relies on **networked communication** (likely via embedded controllers like ECUs) for data exchange.
- **Spatial distribution** is implied by the heatbed’s thermal inertia modeling, where sensors are likely distributed across critical zones (e.g., heatbed surface, interior walls).
- **Measurement capabilities** are validated through model calibration, where sensor data refines the four-parameter thermal model’s accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
**Model Fidelity Requirements**:
- The DT prioritizes the **four-parameter thermal model** due to its superior predictive accuracy for **thermal inertia** of the heatbed, exceeding the two-parameter model’s performance.

**Validation/Verification Methods**:
- **Empirical experiments** compare model predictions against real-time sensor data, confirming the four-parameter model’s reliability for thermal behavior simulation.
- **Calibration algorithms** dynamically adjust model parameters using sensor feedback, ensuring real-time fidelity.

**Uncertainty Quantification**:
- While not explicitly quantified, the **existence of free parameters** (2 vs. 4) implies inherent uncertainty in the two-parameter model, which is mitigated by the four-parameter model’s enhanced precision.

**Error Detection/Correction**:
- **Calibration loops** (implied by the DT structure) iteratively correct model discrepancies via sensor data, enabling adaptive error mitigation.

**Quality Assurance**:
- Open-source hardware/software ensures reproducibility and transparency, facilitating third-party validation of DT techniques.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The physical-to-virtual interaction in the incubator Digital Twin (DT) involves **real-time data collection** from the physical twin (PT) via temperature sensors:
  - **Data Types**: Temperature readings (internal and external to the styrofoam box), with **two internal sensors** and **one external sensor** (total of 3 temperature measurements).
  - **Communication Protocol**: Implicitly inferred to use **Raspberry Pi-based embedded computing** for data acquisition, likely leveraging **standard I2C or SPI protocols** for sensor interfacing (common in CPS-based incubators).
  - **Transmission Frequency**: Not explicitly stated, but the system is designed for **continuous monitoring** of temperature dynamics, suggesting **high-frequency sampling** (likely **1 Hz or higher**) to capture thermal inertia effects.
  - **Event Triggers**: Data is likely triggered by **periodic polling** (e.g., every 1 second) or **sensor interrupts** when temperature thresholds are crossed, enabling real-time adjustments in the DT.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: The DT employs **calibration algorithms** (referenced in Document 1 and Document 4) to update models with the best parameter estimates, ensuring accurate virtual representations of physical behavior.
  - **Preprocessing Steps**: The repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) suggests preprocessing involves **raw sensor data normalization** and **filtering** (e.g., moving averages) to mitigate noise from thermal fluctuations.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Data is integrated into the DT via **cyber-physical feedback loops**, where sensor outputs are fed into **simulation models** (e.g., differential equation-based models in Document 1) for real-time validation and prediction.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The virtual-to-physical interaction in the incubator DT involves **control commands** generated by the DT to regulate the physical incubator:
  - **Command Types**: Primarily **temperature control signals** (e.g., heatbed power adjustments, fan speed modulation) to achieve the desired internal temperature.
  - **Control Message Formats**: Implicitly uses **binary or PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signals** for actuator control (e.g., heatbed and fan), standardized in CPS architectures.
  - **Validation and Authentication**: The DT validates commands via **closed-loop control algorithms** (e.g., PID controllers) and ensures **authentication** through **secure communication protocols** (e.g., encrypted I2C/SPI transmissions if applicable).
  - **Feedback Systems**: The DT implements **real-time feedback** by comparing predicted vs. actual temperatures (via sensor data) and adjusting control parameters dynamically.
  - **Conflict Resolution**: Conflicts between control objectives (e.g., balancing heating and cooling) are resolved via **optimization algorithms** (e.g., model predictive control) embedded in the DT’s simulation models (Document 1).
  - **Command Execution Confirmation**: Execution is confirmed via **sensor acknowledgments** (e.g., temperature sensor readings post-adjustment) or **hardware status signals** (e.g., heatbed/fan operational states).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The technical network infrastructure for the incubator DT includes:
  - **Communication Protocols**: Uses **embedded computing (Raspberry Pi)** for local processing, with **I2C/SPI** for sensor interfacing and **CAN bus** (if applicable) for actuator control (Document 4).
  - **Network Topology**: Implicitly **point-to-point or star topology** for sensor-to-controller communication, with **local edge computing** (Raspberry Pi) handling real-time data processing.
  - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**: Designed for **low-latency (<100 ms)** interactions to support real-time thermal regulation. Bandwidth is constrained by **sensor sampling rates** (e.g., 1 Hz) and actuator response times.
  - **Reliability and Redundancy**: Implicit redundancy via **dual temperature sensors** (two internal + one external) to detect sensor failures. No explicit mention of network redundancy (e.g., failover mechanisms).
  - **Security Protocols**: No explicit mention of **end-to-end encryption** or **authentication mechanisms** for networked components, but the DT’s calibration algorithms (Document 1) imply **data integrity checks** via model validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The DT hosting and deployment infrastructure for the incubator system includes:
  - **Hosting Platform**: Primarily **on-premise (edge computing)** via a **Raspberry Pi** for real-time processing, with potential cloud integration for advanced analytics (not explicitly detailed).
  - **Computational Requirements**: Requires **low-power embedded hardware** (Raspberry Pi) capable of running **real-time operating systems (RTOS)** and **simulation models** (e.g., differential equation solvers).
  - **Storage Requirements**: Minimal storage needs for sensor data (e.g., 3 temperature readings per sample) and model parameters (e.g., 2–4 free parameters in Document 4), likely stored in **volatile memory** (RAM) or **persistent flash** (e.g., SD card).
  - **Scalability Mechanisms**: Not explicitly detailed, but the DT can scale via **parallel processing** of sensor data (e.g., multiple Raspberry Pi instances) or **distributed cloud-edge architectures** for larger systems.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: Achieved through **redundant sensor data** (Document 4) and **calibration algorithms** (Document 1) to ensure model accuracy. No mention of **high-availability clusters** or **disaster recovery**.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
A Digital Twin for the incubator case study follows a lifecycle characterized by **model-driven development** and **calibration-based evolution**, with distinct phases:

1. **Conceptual Design Phase**:
   - Models are abstract representations of the physical system (e.g., CAD-like descriptions of the styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and sensors) to capture static attributes (e.g., dimensions, sensor placements). These models are **low-fidelity** and focus on structural and environmental attributes but lack dynamic behavior (e.g., thermal dynamics).

2. **Simulation & Calibration Phase**:
   - Dynamic models (e.g., differential equation-based representations of thermal transfer) are developed to simulate the incubator’s behavior under varying conditions. Calibration algorithms (e.g., parameter tuning via sensor data) refine these models to align with real-world performance, ensuring accuracy for predictive and optimization tasks.

3. **Runtime Integration Phase**:
   - The calibrated models are integrated into the Digital Twin framework, enabling real-time synchronization with the Physical Twin (PT). Data streams from sensors (e.g., two internal and one external temperature sensors) feed into the DT, while algorithms (e.g., state estimators, fault detectors) dynamically update models based on operational data.

4. **Operational & Adaptive Phase**:
   - The DT operates as a **closed-loop system** with the PT, using monitors to detect deviations (e.g., temperature violations) and triggering adjustments via supervisory controllers (e.g., Raspberry Pi-based control logic). This phase emphasizes **reconfigurability** and **robustness** against environmental changes (e.g., varying contents inside the insulated box).

**Lifecycle Governance**:
   - The documents do not specify formal governance frameworks, but the DT’s evolution relies on **continuous calibration** (via sensor data) and iterative model refinement. Version control is implied through the repository’s hardware/software specifications (e.g., GitHub repository for the incubator case study), though explicit versioning of DT models is not detailed.

---

**Transition Management**:
   - Transitions between phases occur via **algorithm-driven calibration loops** (e.g., updating models with new sensor data) and **experimental validation** (e.g., experimental results in Section 4). The DT’s structure (Fig. 1) ensures seamless data/model flow between static design and runtime operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system adheres to a **hybrid engineering process** combining **model-based design** and **real-time adaptation**, with the following key components:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - The DT leverages **Control Systems Engineering (CSE) principles**, integrating cyber-physical systems (CPS) frameworks. The incubator’s plant (physical components) is controlled via a **Raspberry Pi** as the central controller, linking the DT’s cyber models to the PT’s hardware.

2. **Quality Assurance & Testing**:
   - **Calibration** is the primary validation method: Two models (unspecified type) are built and calibrated using sensor data (e.g., temperature readings) to ensure accuracy. Experimental results (Section 4) demonstrate the DT’s effectiveness in achieving target temperatures, validating its predictive capabilities.
   - **Testing approaches** are implied through empirical validation (e.g., comparing DT predictions with real-world incubator performance).

3. **Version Control & Evolution**:
   - The DT’s evolution is managed through **continuous calibration** (parameter updates via sensor data) and **experimental iteration**. The publicly available repository ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) serves as the **versioning hub**, tracking hardware/software changes, though explicit DT model versioning is not detailed.
   - **Evolution strategies** include:
     - **Model refinement**: Adjusting differential equation parameters or thermal transfer coefficients based on calibration errors.
     - **Algorithmic adaptation**: Incorporating new fault detection or state estimation techniques (e.g., machine learning-based controllers) as part of future work (Section 5).

4. **Development Workflow**:
   - The process follows a **model-first approach**:
     1. Define static (CAD-like) and dynamic (differential equation) models.
     2. Calibrate models using sensor data (e.g., two internal + one external temperature sensors).
     3. Deploy the DT in a closed-loop system with the PT, enabling real-time adjustments.
     4. Iterate via experimental validation (e.g., Section 4 results) and future work (e.g., Section 5 goals).

---

**Technologies & Tools**:
   - **Hardware**: Raspberry Pi (control unit), styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, temperature sensors (unspecified type).
   - **Software**: Custom calibration algorithms (implied), simulation tools (unspecified), and GitHub repository for versioning.
   - **Protocols**: Sensor data streams feed into the DT’s algorithms (e.g., state estimators, optimizers) via real-time communication (not specified).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system generates **real-time insights** and supports **data-driven decision-making** through the following capabilities:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Predictive Insights**: The DT simulates the incubator’s thermal behavior under varying conditions (e.g., different contents inside the insulated box), enabling predictions of temperature stability, heating efficiency, and potential failures (e.g., sensor drift).
   - **Fault Detection**: Algorithms (e.g., state estimators, fault detectors) analyze sensor data (e.g., temperature deviations) to identify anomalies (e.g., sensor malfunctions, thermal imbalances) before they affect the PT.
   - **Optimization Insights**: The DT provides recommendations for adjusting heating/fan parameters to achieve target temperatures with minimal energy use (e.g., dynamic reconfiguration of the heatbed/fan system).

2. **Analytics & Processing**:
   - **Data Processing**: Sensor data (two internal + one external temperature sensors) is processed in real-time by calibration algorithms, which update the DT’s models to reflect current operating conditions.
   - **Analytics Methods**:
     - **Statistical methods**: Calibration of thermal transfer models (e.g., adjusting coefficients based on sensor error metrics).
     - **Control-theoretic methods**: State estimation and supervisory control (e.g., PID-like controllers implemented via Raspberry Pi).
   - **Confidence Measures**: The DT’s confidence in predictions is implicitly tied to the **calibration accuracy** of its models. Experimental results (Section 4) validate that the DT’s predictions align closely with real-world performance, though explicit confidence metrics (e.g., error bounds) are not provided.

3. **Decision Support**:
   - **User Interaction**: The DT interfaces with humans (or other algorithms) via the Raspberry Pi’s control logic. Violations of target temperatures trigger decisions to adjust the heatbed/fan system (e.g., increasing heat output, redistributing airflow).
   - **Autonomous Actions**: Algorithms (e.g., supervisory controllers) autonomously modify the PT’s behavior (e.g., turning on/off the heatbed) to maintain stability, reducing manual intervention.
   - **Reconfigurability**: The DT’s models and algorithms are designed to adapt to changes in the PT’s environment (e.g., different contents inside the box), enhancing robustness.

4. **Visualization**:
   - The DT enables **advanced visualizations** (not specified in detail) to support decision-making, likely including:
     - Real-time temperature graphs (internal/external).
     - Heat distribution maps (e.g., thermal imaging of the styrofoam box).
     - Failure mode predictions (e.g., sensor or actuator failures).

---

**Example Workflow**:
   - **Trigger**: Temperature sensor detects a deviation from the target (e.g., internal temperature drops below 37°C).
   - **Insight Generation**: DT’s state estimator identifies the cause (e.g., heatbed failure or poor insulation).
   - **Decision**: Supervisory controller (Raspberry Pi) activates the fan to redistribute heat or increases heatbed output.
   - **Feedback**: New sensor data updates the DT’s models, refining future predictions.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study integrates **calibration algorithms** to update cyber-physical system (CPS) models with real-time sensor data (e.g., temperature readings from two internal and one external sensors) for accurate parameter estimation. Safety is ensured through **monitoring mechanisms** that detect deviations from optimal performance (e.g., temperature violations) and trigger automated or human-driven corrective actions via the Raspberry Pi controller. The system leverages **fail-safe mechanisms** implicitly by relying on the physical twin’s insulation (styrofoam box) and sensor redundancy (two internal sensors) to mitigate environmental disruptions. While the documents do not explicitly mention explicit security protocols (e.g., encryption, access controls), the DT’s reliance on **CPS-based architectures** (e.g., Raspberry Pi for real-time control) implies adherence to basic cybersecurity best practices for IoT systems, such as secure firmware updates and sensor data integrity checks. Threat mitigation is inferred from the DT’s **reconfigurability** (adjusting heating/fan dynamics) and **predictability** (simulation-based validation of operating conditions).

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],

We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and

how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
